content:
# Mark Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 32r–32v (PDF pages 63–64) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_002_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_002_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ A few days later, Jesus returned to Capernaum, and word got around that he was home.

² So many people packed into the house that there was no room left—not even outside the door—while he spoke the message to them.

³ Four men arrived, carrying a paralyzed man on a stretcher.

⁴ They couldn't get through the crowd to reach Jesus, so they climbed up on the roof above him, tore it open, and lowered the stretcher with the paralyzed man on it right down in front of him.

⁵ When Jesus saw how much faith they had, he said to the paralyzed man, "My child, your sins are forgiven."

⁶ Some religious scholars were sitting nearby, and in their minds they were thinking,

⁷ "How can he say such a thing? This is blasphemy! Only God has the power to forgive sins."

⁸ Jesus immediately sensed what they were debating in their minds and said to them, "Why are you thinking these things?

⁹ Which is easier—to say to this paralyzed man, 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say, 'Get up, pick up your stretcher, and walk'?

¹⁰ But I want you to know that the Son of Man has the authority to forgive sins right here on earth"—and with that, he turned to the paralyzed man and said,

¹¹ "I'm telling you: Get up, pick up your stretcher, and go home."

¹² The man stood up, grabbed his stretcher, and walked out in full view of everyone. They were absolutely astonished and praised God, saying, "We have never seen anything like this!"

¹³ Jesus went out again to the lakeside. The whole crowd came to him, and he taught them.

¹⁴ As he walked along, he noticed Levi son of Alphaeus sitting at his tax collection booth. "Follow me," Jesus said to him. Levi got up and followed.

¹⁵ Later, while Jesus was having dinner at Levi's house, a large number of tax collectors and other notorious sinners joined him and his disciples at the table—for many such people were following him by now.

¹⁶ When the Pharisees' legal experts saw him eating with sinners and tax collectors, they challenged his disciples: "Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?"

¹⁷ Jesus overheard them and replied, "Healthy people don't need a doctor—sick people do. I didn't come to call those who think they're righteous. I came to call sinners."

¹⁸ The followers of John and the Pharisees were observing a fast. Some people came to Jesus and asked, "John's disciples fast, and so do the Pharisees' disciples. Why don't yours?"

¹⁹ Jesus answered, "Do wedding guests go hungry while the groom is still celebrating with them? Of course not—not while the groom is right there.

²⁰ But a time will come when the groom is taken away from them, and on that day they will fast.

²¹ Nobody patches an old coat with a piece of new, unshrunk fabric. If you try, the new patch will pull away from the old cloth and leave an even bigger hole.

²² And nobody pours fresh wine into stiff old wineskins. The wine would burst the skins, and you'd lose both the wine and the skins. No—fresh wine goes into fresh wineskins."

²³ One Sabbath, Jesus and his disciples were walking through some grain fields, and the disciples began picking heads of grain as they went.

²⁴ The Pharisees objected: "Look at that! Why are they doing something that's not permitted on the Sabbath?"

²⁵ Jesus replied, "Haven't you ever read what David did when he and his companions were hungry and in need?

²⁶ He went into the house of God—this was when Abiathar was high priest—and ate the sacred bread that only priests were allowed to eat. And he shared it with his men."

²⁷ Then he told them, "The Sabbath was made to serve people—people were not made to serve the Sabbath.

²⁸ That is why the Son of Man has authority over even the Sabbath."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Tore it open" (v. 4):** The scene is astonishing. These men literally dug through a roof—a Palestinian roof made of beams, branches, and packed earth—right over Jesus' head while he was teaching. Debris would have rained down. Mark tells this with an almost humorous breathlessness, and I tried to capture that energy.

2. **"My child" (v. 5):** The Greek τέκνον is tender and intimate—not the formal "son" but the affectionate "child." And Jesus says it to a grown man. This moment is pastoral before it becomes theological.

3. **"Notorious sinners" (v. 15):** The Greek ἁμαρτωλοί doesn't just mean people who occasionally sinned—in this social context, these were people classified by the religious establishment as living outside the boundaries of acceptable Jewish life. I added "notorious" to convey the social stigma.

4. **"Don't need a doctor—sick people do" (v. 17):** This is one of Jesus's most cutting replies. The irony is layered: the "healthy" are not actually healthy; the "righteous" are not actually righteous. Jesus lets the Pharisees condemn themselves with their own categories.

5. **"Wedding guests go hungry" (v. 19):** The Greek "sons of the bridal chamber" is an idiom for wedding attendants. I rendered it conversationally because the force of Jesus's argument is rhetorical and common-sense—fasting at a wedding would be absurd.

6. **"The Sabbath was made to serve people" (v. 27):** This is one of the most revolutionary statements in the Gospels. I used "serve" rather than the more abstract "for the sake of" because it captures the functional point Jesus is making—the Sabbath is a gift, not a master.

7. **"Abiathar" (v. 26):** The historical problem (it was Ahimelech, not Abiathar, in 1 Samuel 21) is well-known. I translate faithfully without correcting or explaining away the discrepancy—it is present in the earliest manuscripts.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
